SEMESTRAL WORK - use case diagram
lets revise diagram on this image. make quick overview

Quick overview of the use case diagram (from the image)
System boundary:
“IoT data logger” (the application)
Actors
Admin
Administrative operator of the system.
User
Regular user/consumer of the system.
Device
External IoT device that sends telemetry into the system.
Use cases inside the system
User management
Create/modify/disable users, assign roles, reset credentials, etc.
Device management
Register devices, configure device metadata, assign devices to users, enable/disable devices.
Data logging
Ingest device data (telemetry), validate it, store it.
Visualize data
View charts/tables, browse history, filter by time/device, basic analytics.
Associations (who interacts with what)
Admin → User management
Admin → Device management
Device → Data logging
User → Visualize data
Notes for revision (small but important clarifications)
Keep “Data logging” strictly as device-to-system ingestion (as you drew).
If users/admin also need to export/view raw logs, add a separate UC like “View/export logs” (don’t mix it into “Data logging”).
If login/authorization is in scope, add “Authenticate” and link it to Admin/User (often via <<include>>).
If device onboarding is in scope, consider splitting Device management into:
Register device
Configure device
Assign device to user
If you want, I can rewrite this as a clean PlantUML use case diagram that matches the same layout (Admin left-top, User left-bottom, Device right, four UCs stacked inside the system).

make plant uml diagram

@startuml
left to right direction

skinparam shadowing false
skinparam linetype ortho
skinparam packageStyle rectangle
skinparam usecase {
  BackgroundColor White
  BorderColor Black
}
skinparam actor {
  BorderColor Black
  FontColor Black
}

' Actors (positions similar to sketch)
actor Admin as A
actor User  as U
actor Device as D

rectangle "IoT data logger" as SYS {

  ' --- Use cases stacked vertically (top -> bottom) ---
  usecase "User\nmanagement"  as UC_UM
  usecase "Device\nmanagement" as UC_DM
  usecase "Data\nlogging"     as UC_DL
  usecase "Visualize\ndata"   as UC_VD

  ' Force vertical stacking
  UC_UM -down- UC_DM
  UC_DM -down- UC_DL
  UC_DL -down- UC_VD
}

' --- Associations (as in the image) ---
A -- UC_UM
A -- UC_DM

U -- UC_VD

D -- UC_DL

@enduml

left to right direction

  usecase "User\nmanagement"   as UC_UM
  usecase "Device\nmanagement" as UC_DM
  usecase "Data\nlogging"      as UC_DL

  UC_DM  ..> UC_AUTH : <<include>>
  UC_VD  ..> UC_AUTH : <<include>>

A -- UC_DM

U -- UC_VD

D -- UC_DL

for user data visualization include authentication check

Here is the corrected PlantUML diagram where only “Visualize data” includes Authentication check (as requested), matching the structured layout style:
@startuml
left to right direction

skinparam shadowing false
skinparam linetype ortho
skinparam packageStyle rectangle
skinparam actorStyle stickman
skinparam usecase {
  BackgroundColor White
  BorderColor Black
}

' ===== Actors (left side) =====
actor Admin as A
actor User as U
A -down-[hidden]-> U

' ===== Actor (right side) =====
actor Device as D

' ===== System boundary =====
rectangle "IoT data logger" {

  usecase "User\nmanagement" as UC_UM
  usecase "Device\nmanagement" as UC_DM
  usecase "Data\nlogging" as UC_DL
  usecase "Visualize\ndata" as UC_VD
  usecase "Authentication\ncheck" as UC_AUTH

  ' Vertical stacking
  UC_UM -down- UC_DM
  UC_DM -down- UC_DL
  UC_DL -down- UC_VD
  UC_VD -down- UC_AUTH

  ' Include relation ONLY for visualization
  UC_VD ..> UC_AUTH : <<include>>
}

' ===== Associations =====
A -- UC_UM
A -- UC_DM

U -- UC_VD

D -- UC_DL

@enduml
This means:
A User must be authenticated before accessing Visualize data.
Admin management actions remain independent (unless you want to formalize admin authentication too).
Device logs data without authentication (assuming device-level trust or token-based registration outside scope).
If you want stricter modeling (e.g., all human actors require authentication), we can refactor it cleanly using a generalized Authenticate user use case.

Correct — that syntax is invalid in some PlantUML versions.
To vertically align actors on the left side, use a hidden link without direction keyword:

A -[hidden]- U
Here is the corrected full diagram:
@startuml
left to right direction

skinparam shadowing false
skinparam linetype ortho
skinparam packageStyle rectangle
skinparam actorStyle stickman
skinparam usecase {
  BackgroundColor White
  BorderColor Black
}

' ===== Actors (left side) =====
actor Admin as A
actor User as U
A -[hidden]- U

' ===== Actor (right side) =====
actor Device as D

' ===== System boundary =====
rectangle "IoT data logger" {

  usecase "User\nmanagement" as UC_UM
  usecase "Device\nmanagement" as UC_DM
  usecase "Data\nlogging" as UC_DL
  usecase "Visualize\ndata" as UC_VD
  usecase "Authentication\ncheck" as UC_AUTH

  ' Vertical stacking
  UC_UM -down- UC_DM
  UC_DM -down- UC_DL
  UC_DL -down- UC_VD
  UC_VD -down- UC_AUTH

  ' Include relation (only for visualization)
  UC_VD ..> UC_AUTH : <<include>>
}

' ===== Associations =====
A -- UC_UM
A -- UC_DM

U -- UC_VD

D -- UC_DL

@enduml
If you want strict vertical stacking (Admin above User), we can force it using together {} or by switching to top to bottom direction.

actors are declared before the system rectangle,
associations are written inside the rectangle block (so PlantUML naturally places external actors around the system),
and you don’t try to “force” layout with hidden links.

left to right direction

actor User
actor Device

  Admin -- (User management)
  Admin -- (Device management)

  Device -- (Data logging)

  User -- (Visualize data)
  (Visualize data) .> (Authentication check) : <<include>>
}

@enduml

what could be extenend in my use case diagram?

Good question. Right now your diagram models only the core functional flow. For a semestral project, it is somewhat minimal. Below is a structured analysis of what you could extend — divided into functional, cross-cutting, and device-side improvements.
1️⃣ Functional Extensions (Most Important)
A. Authentication & Authorization (properly modeled)
Currently:
Visualize data <<include>> Authentication check
You could extend to:
Log in
Log out
Register
Reset password
Role-based access control
Example improvements:
(User management) .> (Authenticate) : <<include>>
(Device management) .> (Authenticate) : <<include>>
(Visualize data) .> (Authenticate) : <<include>>
Or better:
Add "Authorize access" as a separate use case.
B. Device Lifecycle Management
Instead of one generic "Device management", split it:
Register device
Configure device
Assign device to user
Update firmware
Disable / delete device
Monitor device status
This gives your system more architectural clarity.
C. Data Operations (currently too simplified)
Right now:
Data logging
You could decompose into:
Receive telemetry
Validate data
Store data
Detect anomaly
Trigger alert
Aggregate data
Example:
(Data logging) .> (Validate data) : <<include>>
(Data logging) .> (Store data) : <<include>>
(Data logging) .> (Trigger alert) : <<extend>>
That shows optional behavior (good UML modeling).
2️⃣ User-Side Extensions
Your "Visualize data" is too general.
You could split into:

View dashboard
Filter data
Export data (CSV / PDF)
Compare time ranges
Configure alerts
Receive notification
Example:
(Visualize data) .> (Filter data) : <<include>>
(Visualize data) .> (Export data) : <<extend>>
3️⃣ Admin Extensions
Admin usually has more power:
Manage roles
Audit logs
System configuration
View system statistics
Backup / restore database
This significantly improves academic quality.
4️⃣ External System Actors (very strong improvement)
Currently only:
Device
You could add:
Database (if treated as external system)
Notification service (Email/SMS)
Cloud storage
Authentication provider
MQTT broker
Example:
actor "MQTT Broker"
actor "Notification Service"
This makes the architecture more realistic.
5️⃣ Non-Functional Use Cases (advanced modeling)
If you want high-level modeling:
System monitoring
Logging & auditing
Data encryption
Backup & recovery
These are often required in semestral projects.
6️⃣ Use of <<extend>> (you currently don’t use it)
You only use <<include>>.
A good UML diagram should use both.
Good candidates for <<extend>>:

Trigger alert extends Data logging
Export data extends Visualize data
Multi-factor authentication extends Authenticate
Password reset extends Authenticate
7️⃣ Structural Improvement Suggestion
Your current diagram models:
Admin → Management
User → Visualization
Device → Logging
But you are missing:
Admin visualizing data?
Admin managing users via authentication?
User managing their own profile?
Clarify actor responsibilities.
Recommended Academic-Level Version (Clean and Strong)
Minimal but strong version:
Actors:

Admin
User
Device
Notification Service
Use cases:
Authenticate
User management
Device management
Data logging
Validate data
Store data
Trigger alert
Visualize data
Export data
Relations:
Includes for mandatory flows
Extends for optional behavior
This would make your diagram architecturally complete without becoming chaotic.
If you want, I can now:
Propose a balanced final version suitable for semestral submission
Or generate a fully extended professional version
Or analyze it from UML correctness perspective (formal modeling review)
Tell me the expected academic depth.

make Export data extends Visualize data extend

Correct modeling:
Visualize data = base use case
Export data = optional behavior
Therefore → Export data <<extend>> Visualize data
Here is the clean PlantUML in your established layout style:
@startuml
left to right direction

actor Admin
actor User
actor Device

rectangle "IoT data logger" {

  Admin -- (User management)
  Admin -- (Device management)

  Device -- (Data logging)

  User -- (Visualize data)

  (Visualize data) .> (Authentication check) : <<include>>

  (Export data) ..> (Visualize data) : <<extend>>
}

@enduml
Semantics
Authentication is mandatory for visualization → <<include>>
Export is optional behavior during visualization → <<extend>>
This is formally correct UML modeling.
If you want it academically precise, we can also add an extension point label inside Visualize data.

how can i affect layout in plantuml?

PlantUML layout control is heuristic-based (Graphviz underneath). You cannot manually place elements with coordinates, but you can strongly influence layout using structural and directional constraints.
Below is a precise overview of what actually works.

1️⃣ Global Direction Control
Horizontal flow (most common for Use Case diagrams)
left to right direction
Vertical flow
top to bottom direction
This affects how PlantUML tries to organize nodes.
2️⃣ Element Declaration Order (Very Important)
PlantUML places elements roughly in the order they are declared.
To force:

Actors left
System middle
Device right
Declare in this order:
actor Admin
actor User

rectangle "System" {
   ...
}

actor Device
Declaration order alone often fixes 70% of layout issues.
3️⃣ Associations Inside the Rectangle (Strongest Trick)
If you write:
rectangle System {
   Admin -- (UC1)
}
PlantUML automatically places:
Admin outside
UC inside
Direction determined by link
This is the cleanest way to control left/right actor positioning.
4️⃣ Hidden Links (Layout Anchors)
Use invisible links to force relative placement.
Horizontal anchor
Admin -[hidden]-> System
System -[hidden]-> Device
Vertical anchor
UC1 -[hidden]-> UC2
This forces ordering without visible connections.
5️⃣ Together Grouping
Keeps elements at same level:
together {
   actor Admin
   actor User
}
Useful when PlantUML splits actors unexpectedly.
6️⃣ Forcing Vertical Stack of Use Cases
If you want stacked UCs inside system:
(UC1)
(UC2)
(UC3)
Then optionally:
(UC1) -[hidden]-> (UC2)
(UC2) -[hidden]-> (UC3)
Do NOT use real links if you don't want semantic connection.
7️⃣ Linetype and Routing
Cleaner academic diagrams:
skinparam linetype ortho
Prevents diagonal spaghetti lines.
8️⃣ Package vs Rectangle
Sometimes package behaves better than rectangle:
package "IoT data logger" {
   ...
}
Graphviz sometimes aligns packages more predictably.
9️⃣ When Layout Refuses to Cooperate
PlantUML cannot:
Pin absolute coordinates
Guarantee perfect symmetry
Lock exact spacing
It is constraint-based, not manually positioned.
If you need pixel-perfect layout → use draw.io, Visio, Figma.

🔟 Best Practice for Use Case Diagrams
For stable left–center–right structure:
@startuml
left to right direction

actor Admin
actor User
actor Device

rectangle "System" {

  Admin -- (UC1)
  User -- (UC2)
  Device -- (UC3)

}

@enduml
This is the most stable structure.
⚠️ Common Mistakes
Connecting use cases just to force stacking (wrong modeling)
Overusing hidden arrows everywhere
Mixing direction statements mid-diagram
Trying to micromanage layout
If You Want
I can:
Show you a layout-optimized template for semestral work
Or analyze your exact diagram and tell you why Graphviz is resisting your layout
Or explain how PlantUML internally ranks nodes (advanced topic)
Tell me how deep you want to go.

how to define the use case in rectangle but not the connection yet?

In PlantUML, you can declare (define) use cases inside the system boundary first, and create the associations later (outside the rectangle block).
That is completely valid UML and often improves layout control.

✅ Correct Pattern
Step 1 — Define system + use cases only
@startuml
left to right direction

actor Admin
actor User
actor Device

rectangle "IoT data logger" {

  usecase UC_UM as "User management"
  usecase UC_DM as "Device management"
  usecase UC_DL as "Data logging"
  usecase UC_VD as "Visualize data"
  usecase UC_AUTH as "Authentication check"

}
At this point:
Use cases are defined
No relationships exist yet
Layout is cleaner
Step 2 — Add associations afterwards
Admin -- UC_UM
Admin -- UC_DM
User -- UC_VD
Device -- UC_DL

UC_VD ..> UC_AUTH : <<include>>
🔎 Why This Is Good Practice
Separation of structure and behavior
Easier layout debugging
Cleaner modeling
Better readability in large diagrams
You can reorder associations without breaking grouping
🧠 Important Detail
If you define a use case without alias:
(User management)
You cannot easily reference it later.
Better:

usecase UC_UM as "User management"
Then reference via UC_UM.
📌 Full Clean Example
@startuml
left to right direction

actor Admin
actor User
actor Device

rectangle "IoT data logger" {

  usecase UC_UM as "User management"
  usecase UC_DM as "Device management"
  usecase UC_DL as "Data logging"
  usecase UC_VD as "Visualize data"
  usecase UC_AUTH as "Authentication check"

}

Admin -- UC_UM
Admin -- UC_DM
User -- UC_VD
Device -- UC_DL

UC_VD ..> UC_AUTH : <<include>>

@enduml
If you'd like, I can also explain:
Difference between defining with usecase keyword vs (Name)
When to use package instead of rectangle
How aliasing affects layout ranking internally

You’re right — in the “define first, connect later” example I included only the <<include>> and omitted the <<extend>>.
Here’s the corrected full version with both:

Visualize data <<include>> Authentication check
Export data <<extend>> Visualize data
@startuml
left to right direction

actor Admin
actor User
actor Device

rectangle "IoT data logger" {

  usecase UC_UM   as "User management"
  usecase UC_DM   as "Device management"
  usecase UC_DL   as "Data logging"
  usecase UC_VD   as "Visualize data"
  usecase UC_AUTH as "Authentication check"
  usecase UC_EXP  as "Export data"
}

' --- Associations (added after definitions) ---
Admin  -- UC_UM
Admin  -- UC_DM
User   -- UC_VD
Device -- UC_DL

' --- Relationships ---
UC_VD  ..> UC_AUTH : <<include>>
UC_EXP ..> UC_VD   : <<extend>>

@enduml
If you want it academically strict, we can also add an extension point label inside Visualize data (optional in UML, but sometimes required by teachers).

make thesis on how to put actors to right side on plant uml

Thesis
Controlling Actor Placement on the Right Side in PlantUML Use Case Diagrams
Abstract
PlantUML does not support absolute positioning of diagram elements. Instead, layout is computed using Graphviz’s ranking and constraint algorithms. This document explains how to reliably position actors on the right side of a Use Case diagram using structural constraints, declaration order, association direction, and layout anchoring techniques.
1. The Core Problem
PlantUML:
Does not allow coordinate-based placement.
Uses a constraint-based graph layout engine.
Determines left/right placement based on:
Declaration order
Link direction
Graph ranking
Edge constraints
Therefore, “put actor on right” is not a command — it is a structural modeling task.
2. Primary Strategy: Structural Direction Control
2.1 Global Direction
Always define:
left to right direction
This instructs Graphviz to rank nodes horizontally.
Without this, vertical ranking dominates.

3. Declaration Order (Critical Factor)
Graphviz tends to place earlier elements to the left.
To position an actor on the right:

actor Admin
actor User

rectangle "System" {
   ...
}

actor Device
Device declared last → tends to be placed right.
This is not guaranteed, but highly influential.

4. Strongest Method: Association Inside Rectangle
The most stable method is to define associations inside the system boundary:
@startuml
left to right direction

actor Admin
actor User
actor Device

rectangle "System" {

  Admin -- (Manage users)
  User -- (View data)
  Device -- (Send data)

}
@enduml
Why this works:
Actors connected from the left side of use cases → appear left
Actors connected from the right side → appear right
Graphviz distributes external nodes around the rectangle
This is the most reliable technique.
5. Hidden Layout Anchors (Advanced Control)
When layout still resists:
5.1 Horizontal Anchor
Admin -[hidden]-> System
System -[hidden]-> Device
This creates invisible directional ranking.
Effect:

Admin left of System
Device right of System
No visible connection is drawn.
5.2 Directional Hidden Links
System -[hidden]-> Device
This explicitly ranks Device after System.
Useful when PlantUML keeps pulling actors left.

6. Together Grouping (Stability Improvement)
If multiple right-side actors must align:
together {
   actor Device
   actor "Notification Service"
}
Prevents separation during layout solving.
7. Rank Enforcement (Graphviz Behavior Insight)
Graphviz ranks nodes based on:
Edge direction
Edge count
Subgraph boundaries
Edge weight (implicit)
To push an actor right:
Minimize left-side connections
Avoid cross connections
Ensure edges flow left → right
Example of problematic structure:
Device -- (UC)
(UC) -- Admin
This creates a bidirectional ranking conflict.
8. Clean Right-Side Template (Recommended)
@startuml
left to right direction
skinparam linetype ortho

actor Admin
actor User

rectangle "IoT data logger" as SYS {

  usecase UC1 as "User management"
  usecase UC2 as "Device management"
  usecase UC3 as "Data logging"
}

actor Device

Admin -- UC1
Admin -- UC2
User  -- UC3
Device -- UC3

SYS -[hidden]-> Device

@enduml
This ensures:
Admin/User left
System center
Device right
9. Why Actors Sometimes Refuse to Move Right
Common causes:
Associations defined outside rectangle in wrong order
Mixed directional statements
Missing left to right direction
Symmetrical constraints
Cross-links between actors
PlantUML optimizes layout — not semantic intent.
10. What Cannot Be Done
PlantUML does NOT support:
Absolute coordinates
Fixed pixel positions
Manual drag placement
Hard “place on right” commands
If exact placement is required → use draw.io, Visio, Figma.
11. Formal Recommendation for Academic Diagrams
For semestral work:
Use left to right direction
Declare right-side actors last
Define associations inside the rectangle
Avoid artificial linking between use cases
Use hidden anchors only when necessary
Keep diagram semantically correct (no fake edges)
Conclusion
Placing actors on the right side in PlantUML is not a positional operation but a structural modeling task. The most robust method combines:
Horizontal direction control
Proper declaration order
Association placement inside the system boundary
Optional hidden ranking constraints
Understanding Graphviz’s constraint-based layout model is essential for predictable results.
If you want, I can now:
Provide a minimal deterministic right-side template
Explain Graphviz ranking internals in depth
Or analyze why your exact diagram fails to place Device on the right

@startuml
left to right direction
skinparam packageStyle rectangle

actor Admin
actor User

rectangle "IoT data logger" {

  usecase UC_UM   as "User management"
  usecase UC_DM   as "Device management"
  usecase UC_DL   as "Data logging"
  usecase UC_VD   as "Visualize data"
  usecase UC_AUTH as "Authentication check"
  usecase UC_EXP  as "Export data"


  UC_VD  .left.> UC_AUTH : <<include>>
  UC_EXP .left.> UC_VD   : <<extend>>

  UC_UM -left[hidden]-> UC_DM
  UC_DM -left[hidden]-> UC_DL
  UC_DL -left[hidden]-> UC_VD
  UC_VD -left[hidden]-> UC_AUTH
  UC_AUTH -left[hidden]-> UC_EXP
}

actor Device

Admin  -- UC_UM
Admin  -- UC_DM
User   -- UC_VD
UC_DL -- Device


@enduml

based on this diagram make text description of use cases and actors for the iot data logger system.
Below is a structured textual specification of actors and use cases derived from your UML Use Case Diagram of the IoT Data Logger system. The description follows a common SRS / UML use-case documentation format used in software engineering (actors → use cases with purpose and relationships).
IoT Data Logger System – Actors and Use Cases Description
1. Actors
1.1 Admin
Description:
The Admin is a privileged user responsible for configuring and maintaining the IoT data logger system.
Responsibilities

Manage system users.
Configure and manage registered IoT devices.
Ensure proper operation of the data collection infrastructure.
Interactions with the system
User management
Device management
Access level
Full administrative access.
1.2 User
Description:
The User is a standard system user who interacts with the data logger primarily for accessing collected data.
Responsibilities

View collected data.
Analyze data through system visualization tools.
Optionally export data for external analysis.
Interactions with the system
Visualize data
Access level
Read-only access to measurement data.
1.3 Device
Description:
The Device represents an external IoT sensor or embedded system that sends measurement data to the IoT data logger.
Responsibilities

Generate measured data.
Send measurement data to the system.
Interactions with the system
Data logging
Access level
External system entity (not a human user).
2. Use Case Descriptions
UC_UM — User Management
Primary actor: Admin
Description:
This use case allows the administrator to manage user accounts in the system.

Typical actions

Create new user accounts
Modify user permissions
Remove users
Reset credentials
Goal
Ensure controlled access to the IoT data logger system.
UC_DM — Device Management
Primary actor: Admin
Description:
Allows administrators to configure and maintain IoT devices connected to the system.

Typical actions

Register a new device
Configure device parameters
Disable or remove devices
Monitor device status
Goal
Maintain a correct configuration of connected IoT devices.
UC_DL — Data Logging
Primary actor: Device
Description:
This use case represents the process of collecting and storing measurement data from IoT devices.

Typical actions

Device sends measurement data
System validates incoming data
Data are stored in the system database
Goal
Persist measurement data for later analysis and visualization.
UC_VD — Visualize Data
Primary actor: User
Description:
This use case allows users to explore and analyze stored data through graphical visualization.

Typical actions

Select device or data source
Choose time range
Display charts or tables
Goal
Enable users to interpret collected measurement data.
Relationships

Include:

Authentication check
UC_AUTH — Authentication Check
Type: Included use case
Description:
This use case ensures that the user is authenticated and authorized before accessing protected system functionality.

Typical actions

Verify login session
Validate user credentials
Check permissions
Goal
Protect system resources and ensure authorized access.
Used by

Visualize data
UC_EXP — Export Data
Primary actor: User
Description:
This use case enables users to export visualized measurement data into external formats.

Typical actions

Select dataset
Choose export format (CSV, JSON, etc.)
Download file
Goal
Allow external processing and archiving of collected data.
Relationship

Extend:

Visualize data
This means exporting data is an optional action performed after or during data visualization.
3. Relationships Between Use Cases
Include Relationship
Visualize data → Authentication check
The system must verify user authentication before data can be displayed.

Extend Relationship
Export data → Visualize data
Exporting data is an optional functionality that extends the visualization process.

4. System Boundary
The IoT Data Logger system provides the following functional areas:
User administration
Device configuration
Data acquisition
Data visualization
Data export
External entities interacting with the system are:
Admin
User
Device
If you want, I can also convert this into a formal Use Case Specification table format (preconditions, main scenario, alternative flows) that is typically required in software engineering documentation or university assignments.
make it as sentences - paragraph
The IoT Data Logger system interacts with three primary actors: Admin, User, and Device. Each actor represents an external entity that communicates with the system in order to perform specific tasks related to data collection, management, and analysis.
The Admin actor represents a privileged system user responsible for the administration and configuration of the system. The administrator manages user accounts and connected IoT devices to ensure the system operates correctly. Through the User Management use case, the Admin can create, modify, or delete user accounts and manage their permissions. Through the Device Management use case, the Admin can register new IoT devices, configure their parameters, monitor their status, and remove devices that are no longer needed. These administrative functions allow the system infrastructure and access control to be maintained properly.

The User actor represents a standard user of the system who primarily interacts with collected data. The user accesses the Visualize Data use case, which allows them to view and analyze measurement data that has been collected by IoT devices. Within this functionality, the user can select a device, specify a time range, and display the data in graphical or tabular form. Before the system allows the user to access visualization features, the Authentication Check use case is performed. This ensures that the user is properly authenticated and authorized to access the requested data.

The Device actor represents an external IoT sensor or measurement unit connected to the system. Devices interact with the system through the Data Logging use case. In this process, devices send measured data to the system, which receives the data, validates it, and stores it in a database for later use. This stored data becomes available for visualization and further analysis by system users.

In addition to viewing data, users may also export the collected measurements using the Export Data use case. This functionality allows users to download selected datasets in formats such as CSV or JSON for further external processing or archiving. The export functionality is modeled as an extension of the Visualize Data use case, meaning that exporting data is an optional action that can be performed after or during the data visualization process.

Together, these actors and use cases define the core functionality of the IoT Data Logger system, which includes user administration, device configuration, automated data collection from IoT devices, secure access to stored data, data visualization, and optional export of measurement results.
